title: STANDARD FOR COOKED CURED PORK SHOULDER CXS 97-1981 Adopted in 1981. Revised in 1991, 2014, 2015. Amended in 2019, 2022.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR COOKED CURED PORK SHOULDER CXS 97-1981 Adopted in 1981. Revised in 1991, 2014, 2015. Amended in 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Document title: Standard for Cooked Cured Pork Shoulder with adoption/revision years noted (adopted 1981; revised 1991, 2014, 2015; amended 2019, 2022).

Notes a 2022 amendment to the standard (made after the Codex Commission session) replacing prior text on labelling of non-retail containers so that labelling should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Applies to products designated "Cooked Pork Shoulder" in suitable packaging and excludes products with different compositional characteristics unless they carry a qualifying statement that accurately describes the true nature so as not to mislead or cause confusion with products covered by this Standard.

Specifies that cooked pork shoulder must be made from fore-leg meat with bones/cartilage/tendons/ligaments removed (skin and fat optional), be cured and may be smoked/spiced/flavoured, and that heat treatment, cure type and packaging must ensure no public health hazard and wholesomeness under storage, transport and sale.

Lists essential ingredients (uncured pork shoulder; brine of water, food-grade salt and sodium/potassium nitrite), optional ingredients (various sugars, spices, hydrolyzed protein, gelatin), requires ingredients suitable for human consumption and a clean, uniformly cured final product capable of being sliced, and sets meat‑protein requirements (average >17.5% on fat‑free basis; minimum 16.0% absolute).
Specifies essential ingredients for Cooked Pork Shoulder: uncured pork shoulder and a brine of water, food‑grade salt and sodium or potassium nitrite.

Lists permitted optional ingredients for the product, including various sugars and syrups (sucrose, invert sugar, dextrose, lactose, maltose, glucose syrup, honey), spices/seasonings/condiments, water‑soluble aromatic hydrolyzed protein, and food‑grade gelatine.

Sets essential quality factors: raw materials must be suitable for human consumption and free from objectionable odours and flavours, and the final product must be clean, substantially free from staining/container contamination, uniformly and thoroughly cured, and capable of being sliced.
Requires that ingredients (raw materials) be of a quality suitable for human consumption and free from objectionable odours and flavours.
Requires the final product to be clean and substantially free from staining and container contamination, with meat uniformly and thoroughly cured and capable of being sliced.

Sets meat‑protein requirements on a fat‑free basis: average ≥ 17.5% and minimum (absolute) ≥ 16.0%; for canned products the meat‑protein percentage is calculated on the can’s total content and corrected for added gelatine (see Sub‑section 8.1).

Permits preservatives and humectants in accordance with GSFA Tables 1 and 2 for heat‑treated processed meat (category 08.2.2) and allows only specified Table 3 additives as indicated; requires flavourings to follow CXG 66‑2008 and applies GSFA Section 4.1 carry‑over conditions for additives.
Requires compliance with Codex Maximum Levels for contaminants and toxins (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and veterinary drugs.

Recommends preparing and handling the products according to relevant Codex hygiene instruments (e.g., CXC 1‑1969, CXC 58‑2005, CXC 23‑1979, CXG 61‑2007) and states the products should meet any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21‑1997.
States that the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) applies to labelling of these products.

Requires the product name to be "Cooked Pork Shoulder" and, where applicable, include designations (e.g., "with skin", "in/with natural juice", "X added" for gelatine/agar/alginates/carrageenan, "smoked", "smoking agent added"); a declaration describing preparation/processing/presentation must appear with the name if omission would mislead consumers.

Sets date‑marking and storage rules: shelf‑stable products require the year for minimum durability; non‑shelf‑stable products packaged for consumers or catering require day, month and year; non‑shelf‑stable products in non‑retail containers must carry adequate storage and distribution instructions.
Advises that labelling of non‑retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346‑2021).
States that methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234‑1999) relevant to this Standard shall be used to check compliance.
Specifies that when the amount of added gelatine is unknown, 0.5% protein should be deducted from the percentage protein expressed on a fat‑free basis.
